--

How Js Works
What is a JavaScript Engine?
Concept: The JavaScript Engine is a special program that understands and runs JavaScript code.
When you write something like:
console.log("Hello");the computer cannot understand this directly. Computers don’t read English, they don’t read curly braces, and they definitely don’t know what console.log means on their own. Computers only understand machine language — long strings of 0s and 1s that map directly to electrical signals inside the CPU.
So how does your code actually run? That’s where the JavaScript Engine comes in. It acts like a translator. It reads your JavaScript code, breaks it down, converts it into something the machine can actually process, and then hands it off to the computer for execution.
Example
Imagine you speak English and your friend only understands Marathi. You can’t talk directly, so you need a translator standing between you two, converting your words back and forth.
You → Translator → FriendSimilarly:
JavaScript Code → JavaScript Engine → ComputerThe engine sits in the middle of this conversation. It doesn’t just “translate” once and walk away either — as you’ll see later in this post, it parses, analyzes, compiles, executes, and even keeps optimizing your code while it runs. It’s less like a one-time translator and more like a translator who’s also taking notes on which phrases you use most often, so they can translate those faster next time.

Every Browser Has a JavaScript Engine
Whenever you open a website, the browser has to run JavaScript code somewhere — buttons need to respond to clicks, forms need to validate input, animations need to play. Because of this, every browser ships with its own built-in JavaScript engine.

• Chrome → V8

• Firefox → SpiderMonkey

• Safari → JavaScriptCore

• Internet Explorer → Chakra
It’s worth knowing that Microsoft Edge used to run on Chakra too, back when it had its own rendering engine. But Microsoft eventually rebuilt Edge on top of Chromium, which means modern Edge actually runs on V8 now, the same engine that powers Chrome. So technically, Chakra is mostly a legacy engine at this point — still important historically, but no longer the engine behind day-to-day browsing for most Edge users.
Note: Even though these engines have different names, different teams built them, and they’re written by competing companies, they all perform the same fundamental job: executing JavaScript code according to the ECMAScript specification. That’s actually the whole point — the spec is standardized, so the same JS code should behave consistently whether it runs in V8, SpiderMonkey, or JavaScriptCore, even though each engine takes its own approach under the hood to get there.

What is V8?
V8 is the JavaScript engine developed by Google, written in C++, and open-sourced so anyone can study or contribute to it.
It is one of the fastest and most popular JavaScript engines in the world, and it didn’t get that reputation by accident — Google has poured years of engineering effort into making V8 fast, because browser speed has always been a competitive battleground.
It is used in:

• Google Chrome

• Node.js

• Deno

• Electron-based apps (like VS Code and Discord)
console.log("Hello World");Note: When this code runs in Chrome, V8 is responsible for executing it. V8 doesn’t simply interpret your code line by line forever — internally it uses a component called Ignition to quickly turn your code into bytecode and start running it, and a component called TurboFan that watches which parts of your code run repeatedly and compiles those “hot” parts into highly optimized machine code. That combination of “start fast, then get faster” is a big part of why V8 has such a strong reputation for performance.

What is Node.js?
Normally, JavaScript runs inside a browser, tied to a webpage. Node.js broke that assumption — it allows JavaScript to run outside the browser entirely, directly on your machine or on a server.
console.log("Running on server");This can run directly from a terminal using Node.js, with no browser window involved at all. That one shift is what made it possible to write backend servers, command-line tools, build scripts, and even desktop apps entirely in JavaScript — the same language that used to be limited to making webpages interactive.
Under the hood, Node.js takes V8 (the same engine inside Chrome) and pairs it with a library called libuv, which handles things like file access, networking, and timers in a non-blocking way. So V8 handles the “understand and run JavaScript” part, and Node.js wraps extra capabilities around it so that JavaScript can talk to your operating system.
Simple Diagram
JavaScript
|
V
V8
|
----------------
| Chrome |
| Node.js |
----------------
Other JavaScript Engines
Different browsers use different engines, built by different companies, for their own platforms:

• Chrome → V8

• Firefox → SpiderMonkey

• Safari → JavaScriptCore

• Edge → Chakra
A bit of trivia: SpiderMonkey holds a special place in history — it was the very first JavaScript engine ever built, created by Brendan Eich (who also created JavaScript itself) for Netscape back in 1995. Mozilla has continued developing it for Firefox ever since. JavaScriptCore, sometimes called “Nitro,” is the engine inside WebKit and powers Safari on both desktop and iOS. And Chakra was Microsoft’s engine for the old Internet Explorer and the original Edge, before Edge switched over to V8.
Although the names, companies, and internal architectures are all different, their purpose is identical:

• Read JavaScript code

• Understand it

• Execute it

Parts of a JavaScript Engine
A JavaScript engine mainly consists of two important parts:
JavaScript Engine
│
├── Call Stack
│
└── HeapPress enter or click to view image in full size

Js Engine PartsThese two pieces work together constantly while your code runs — one keeps track of what’s currently executing, and the other keeps track of where your data lives. Let’s understand each part one by one.

What is the Call Stack?
The Call Stack is the place where JavaScript executes code, especially functions. It’s where the engine keeps track of “what am I currently doing, and what was I doing before that, so I can come back to it.”
You can think of it as a stack of plates.
Top
-----
plate ← last added, first removed
-----
plate
-----
plate
-----
BottomThe last plate placed on the stack is the first one removed. This principle is called:
LIFO — Last In, First Out
Example
function a() {
b();
}
function b() {
console.log("Hello");
}
a();Execution Flow
Step 1 - a()
Step 2 - b() → a()
Step 3 - console.log() → b() → a()
After - (empty)Notice the order: a() goes onto the stack first, then while a() is still running, b() gets pushed on top of it, and console.log() gets pushed on top of that. Only once console.log() finishes does it get popped off, then b() finishes and gets popped, and finally a() finishes and the stack goes back to empty. That's LIFO in action — whatever was added most recently finishes first.
This is also exactly why you sometimes see a “Maximum call stack size exceeded” error in your console. If a function keeps calling itself without ever stopping (like a recursive function missing a base case), the stack keeps growing taller and taller until it runs out of room — that’s called a stack overflow, and it’s a direct consequence of how the call stack works.
Important: The Call Stack:

• Executes functions

• Keeps track of function calls

• Follows the LIFO principle

What is the Heap?
The Heap is a large, mostly unstructured memory area used to store data. Unlike the Call Stack, which is neat and ordered, the Heap is more like a big open storage space where things get placed wherever there’s room.
It mainly stores:

• Objects

• Arrays

• Large pieces of data
let person = {
name: "John",
age: 20
};The object { name: "John", age: 20 } is stored in the Heap. The variable person itself just holds a reference (essentially an address) pointing to where that object actually lives in the Heap.
Example
Call Stack = Worker → performs the work
Heap = Storage Room → stores the required itemsOne thing worth mentioning here: the Heap doesn’t grow forever. As your program creates objects and arrays, old ones that are no longer being used (nothing in your code references them anymore) become eligible for cleanup. The engine runs a process called garbage collection in the background, which scans the Heap, identifies data that’s no longer reachable, and frees that memory up. This is why JavaScript is described as having “automatic memory management” — you don’t manually allocate or free memory like you would in lower-level languages such as C; the engine handles it for you.

Why Does JavaScript Need Translation?
Computers understand only binary language:
0 1 0 1 1 0 1 0That’s it. At the hardware level, a CPU is just billions of tiny transistors flipping between “on” and “off,” and binary is how we represent those states. But programmers write code like:
console.log("Hello");The computer cannot understand this directly — there’s a massive gap between human-readable syntax and the raw electrical signals a processor responds to. The JavaScript Engine exists specifically to bridge that gap. It translates your code into machine code so that the CPU can actually execute it.
Translation Flow:
JavaScript Code
↓
JavaScript Engine
↓
Machine Code (0s and 1s)
↓
CPU ExecutesThis is also why writing JavaScript feels easy compared to programming directly in machine code — you get to think in terms of variables, functions, and logic, while the engine handles the painful, low-level translation work behind the scenes.

What is Parsing?
Concept: Parsing simply means reading and checking the code.
Before your code can run, the JavaScript Engine has to scan through it character by character, group those characters into meaningful chunks (this first pass is technically called tokenizing or lexical analysis — turning your code into tokens like keywords, identifiers, and symbols), and then check whether everything fits together correctly.
The JavaScript Engine examines the code and verifies:

• Is the syntax correct?

• Are brackets balanced?

• Are keywords used properly?

• Are there any syntax errors?
Example:
// Correct
let x = 10;
// Incorrect - will throw a SyntaxError
let = 10;Note: The parser detects these errors before execution — this is exactly why a typo in one part of your file can cause your entire script to fail immediately, even before any of your code actually runs. The engine refuses to execute anything until it’s confident the structure of the code makes sense.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Parsing → AST → Compilation → Execution → OptimizationThis shows the complete journey of JavaScript code inside the engine, and parsing is the very first checkpoint in that journey.

What is AST (Abstract Syntax Tree)?
Concept: After parsing, JavaScript converts the code into a structure called the Abstract Syntax Tree (AST).
This is a tree-like representation of the code, where each piece of your program becomes a node, and the relationships between those nodes show how the code is structured.
let x = 5 + 3;A simplified AST looks like:
=
/ \
x +
/ \
5 3The AST helps the engine understand:

• Variables

• Operators

• Expressions

• Functions
Note: A tree structure is much easier for the engine to analyze and process than plain text, because it captures meaning and hierarchy rather than just a flat sequence of characters. This isn’t just useful internally either — tools you may have already used, like Babel (which transforms modern JS into older, browser-compatible JS) and ESLint (which checks your code style and flags potential bugs), work by generating and analyzing an AST of your code. So the same concept the engine uses to understand your code is also what powers a lot of the tooling in the Java
